mainEntity.name: What's the cheapest realistic setup for bird photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: An APS-C body plus a used Sigma or Tamron 100-400mm lens — typically £500-£800 all-in used. That gives around 600mm equivalent reach, fast enough autofocus for garden birds, and image quality that embarrasses far more expensive kit.

Not at all — it's a nice-to-have, not a need. Single-point AF has produced world-class bird photography for 20 years. Bird-eye AF shines for birds-in-flight, but for 90% of garden bird work single-point on the eye works just as well.

mainEntity.name: What shutter speed freezes a bird in flight?

acceptedAnswer.text: Small birds: 1/2500s minimum, 1/4000s to be sure. Medium birds: 1/1600s usually enough. Larger birds with slower wingbeats: 1/1000s to 1/1600s. Don't be afraid to push ISO to 3200-6400 — a sharp noisy photo beats a clean blurred one.

mainEntity.name: Where's best to photograph birds if I don't have a garden?

acceptedAnswer.text: Local nature reserves are ideal starting points. WWT sites like Slimbridge and Martin Mere have hides with reliable subjects at known distances. Urban park lakes give you tame waterfowl to practise on. Start somewhere with high encounter rates while you build technique.

Rarely necessary for UK birds. Silhouette, movement and sound matter more. Dark earth-tone clothing is plenty. Move slowly, stop often, keep movements below head height, don't talk. Camo can make other walkers suspicious of you pointing a long lens.

mainEntity.name: Can I use a bridge camera or superzoom?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, for learning and for sharing online. Nikon P1000 and Sony RX10 IV give 1200mm+ reach in one package. Small sensor drops image quality above ISO 800, so can't make big prints. For social media and the joy of the shot, they're genuinely excellent.

mainEntity.name: How do I stop my photos looking like snapshots?

acceptedAnswer.text: Two things distinguish a bird photograph from a bird snapshot: clean background (no branches across the body, no bright bits behind the head) and expressive posture (head turned, catchlight in the eye, mid-movement). Neither requires expensive kit.

mainEntity.name: Is it ethical to use bird feeders to attract subjects?

acceptedAnswer.text: For common garden birds, entirely fine — they benefit from reliable food, especially in winter. Use appropriate food (not bread), keep feeders clean, don't site where cats can ambush. For rare species, baiting is discouraged. Nests of Schedule 1 species require a licence.
